title: Debug & Triage Interview Question Bank
description: Interview preparation covering simulation debug methodology, waveform analysis, protocol triage, and systematic root-cause workflows.

questions.prompt: Your UVM simulation fails with a scoreboard mismatch. What are your first five debug steps?
questions.rubric: Must include: read the error message, check simulation time, trace the failing transaction ID, examine driver vs monitor logs, compare expected vs actual data. Should mention checking for known bugs.
questions.model_answer: 1) Read the full error message — note the simulation time, component path, and transaction details. 2) Search the log for the first error (not the last). 3) Trace the failing transaction from sequence → driver → DUT → monitor → scoreboard. 4) Compare the expected value (from reference model) to the actual value (from monitor). 5) Open the waveform at the failure time and correlate signal-level behavior with the transaction log.

questions.prompt: A simulation hangs at cycle 15000. The waveform shows clocks toggling but no bus activity. How do you triage?
questions.rubric: Check VALID/READY signals, look for stuck state machines, check objection counts, examine reset state, look for missing sequence completion, check for blocking TLM calls.
questions.model_answer: 1) Check bus VALID signals — if all are low, no master is trying to transact; check sequence completion. 2) If VALID is high but READY is low, a slave or interconnect is stalled; identify which component owns READY. 3) Check for circular dependencies (one component waiting for another). 4) Examine the UVM objection count — if it's zero, the phase should have ended. 5) Look at internal state machine registers for stuck states. 6) Check if a blocking TLM get() is waiting on an empty FIFO.

questions.prompt: You see X values propagating through your design after reset. What are common causes and how do you fix them?
questions.rubric: Uninitialized flip-flops, missing reset connections, reset domain crossing, conditional assignments with X control signals, array out-of-bounds, and division by zero.
questions.model_answer: Common causes: 1) Flip-flops not in the reset list of always_ff. 2) Reset signal not connected to a sub-module. 3) Conditional MUX with X on the select (e.g., uninitialized config register). 4) Reading uninitialized memory locations. 5) Combinational paths from unreset registers. Fix: ensure all state elements have explicit reset values, use X-prop simulation modes (Questa -xprop), add assertions to catch X on key control signals early.

questions.prompt: Your functional coverage is stuck at 92%. How do you identify and close the remaining holes?
questions.rubric: Analyze coverage report to identify uncovered bins, classify as unreachable vs under-stimulated, write directed sequences for hard-to-reach states, consider constraint relaxation, check for illegal bin misclassification.
questions.model_answer: 1) Export the coverage report and identify all uncovered bins. 2) Classify each: (a) unreachable by design — mark as exclude_bin with justification. (b) Requires specific sequence — write a directed sequence targeting that scenario. (c) Rare random event — increase iteration count or add constraint bias. 3) Check cross coverage — often specific bin combinations are the gap. 4) Look for illegal bins incorrectly marked as valid. 5) Consider if the coverage model is too fine-grained (e.g., full address range bins when only a few addresses are legal). 6) Track coverage closure rate per seed to ensure the random regressions are still making progress.

questions.prompt: 50 of 200 regression tests fail after a major RTL change. Design your triage workflow.
questions.rubric: Must include: signature grouping, bisect to first failing commit, prioritize by failure type (timeout vs mismatch vs compile), check for common root causes, parallelize investigation, and communicate status.
questions.model_answer: 1) Group failures by signature: compile errors, timeouts, assertion failures, scoreboard mismatches. 2) Fix compile errors first — they cascade. 3) For the largest group, bisect to the RTL commit that introduced them. 4) Check if one root cause explains multiple failures (e.g., a reset change breaking all tests). 5) Prioritize: timeout/hang bugs first (hardest to debug), then data mismatches. 6) Assign sub-groups to team members for parallel investigation. 7) Maintain a triage spreadsheet: test name, failure type, root cause, assigned engineer, status. 8) Daily triage meetings until the count is below threshold. 9) Mark non-reproducible failures as flaky and investigate seed sensitivity.

questions.prompt: How do you decide when a design is ready to tape out from a verification perspective?
questions.rubric: Must discuss: functional coverage closure (not just line/toggle), assertion coverage, formal proof completeness, regression pass rate and stability, known-bug waivers, code coverage analysis, and sign-off metrics.
questions.model_answer: Tape-out readiness requires: 1) Functional coverage at target (typically 100% for critical features, 95%+ overall) with all holes justified. 2) Code coverage (line/branch/toggle/FSM) at 95%+ with uncovered code analyzed for dead code or missing tests. 3) All formal properties proven or bounded to agreed depth. 4) Zero open P0/P1 bugs; all waived bugs documented with risk assessment. 5) Regression pass rate 100% for full regression (no intermittent failures). 6) Coverage rate saturated — additional seeds don't increase coverage. 7) All verification plan items signed off by the verification lead. 8) Cross-domain review: reset/CDC/power-aware checks complete.
